text: A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a flap, dissecting towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the right inguinal and femoral hernia is reduced, followed by the reduction of fat in the right obturator hernia. A three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the space, with dissection extending laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to accommodate the mesh placement.

A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, dissecting towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the right inguinal and femoral hernia is reduced, followed by the reduction of fat in the right obturator hernia. A 3-D mesh is inserted into the space, with dissections carried laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to accommodate the mesh.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision on the left side, entering the preperitoneal space. Dissection is performed laterally, superiorly, inferiorly, and medially towards the symphysis pubis. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the left obturator hernia is reduced, followed by the insertion of a large three-dimensional mesh into the space.

A transverse incision is made on the left side, entering the preperitoneal space. Dissection is performed laterally, superiorly, inferiorly, and medially towards the symphysis pubis. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the left obturator hernia is reduced, and a large three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the space.

The surgeon places the mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers all hernia defects. The mesh is then secured to the abdominal wall using T-seal. Following this, the peritoneum is closed with a two-hole D-lock absorbable suture in a continuous running fashion.

The surgeon lays the mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers all hernia defects. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using T-seal. The peritoneum is then closed with a running two-hole D-lock absorbable suture.
